text:
1. Disconnect the infected server -> <@U047N6LUYJX> 
2. Cut off infrastructure traffic -> <@U047N6LUYJX> 
3. Disconnect DB & freeze backup -> Application
4. Detach from Shared Resource -> <@U047N6LUYJX> 
5. Take snapshots (Disk & Memory) -> <@U047N6LHU0K> 
6. Kill suspicious process or activity -> <@U047N6LUYJX>

elements.text: Disconnect the infected server ->
Cut off infrastructure traffic ->
Disconnect DB & freeze backup -> Application
Detach from Shared Resource ->
Take snapshots (Disk & Memory) ->
Kill suspicious process or activity ->

text:
Steps to be done, mentioned along with the *owning* team
1. Validate backups → <@U06CA3MRAJF> 
2. Wipe infected systems → <@U047N6LUYJX> 
3. Rebuild from clean backup → <@U047N6LUYJX> 
4. Re-enable security agent on the servers → <@U06CA3MRAJF> 
5. Reconnect traffic to services and monitor → <@U047N6LUYJX>

text: phished_email

Here's my observation by going over the comparison
• One is from <mailto:support@clear-tech.com|support@clear-tech.com>, which matches their legitimate domain. The other came from <mailto:updates@cleartech.com|updates@cleartech.com> - note the absence of hyphen in the domain 
• The legit one points to their HTTPS portal requiring login. But the phished one uses urgency, saying 'apply immediately', and links directly to an .exe on <http://cleartech.com>. That's the one Shay clicked 
• This explains the compromise - Shay unknowingly ran the malicious executable disguised as a patch

The legit one points to their HTTPS portal requiring login. But the phished one uses urgency, saying 'apply immediately', and links directly to an .exe on http://cleartech.com. That's the one Shay clicked
This explains the compromise - Shay unknowingly ran the malicious executable disguised as a patch
